records.ety: [F. dbaucher, prob. originally, to entice away from the workshop; pref. d- (L. dis- or de) + OF. bauche, bauge, hut, cf. F. bauge lair of a wild boar; prob. from G. or Icel., cf. Icel. bālkr. See Balk, n.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Debauched (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Debauching.]

defs: To lead away from purity or excellence; to corrupt in character or principles; to mar; to vitiate; to pollute; to seduce; as, to debauch one's self by intemperance; to debauch a woman; to debauch an army.
quotes: A man must have got his conscience thoroughly debauched and hardened before he can arrive to the height of sin.
Her pride debauched her judgment and her eyes.

defs: Excess in eating or drinking; intemperance; drunkenness; lewdness; debauchery.
quotes: The first physicians by debauch were made.

defs: An act or occasion of debauchery.
quotes: Silenus, from his night's debauch, Fatigued and sick.
